critique.summary: The article reports that Ubuntu 26.04 LTS will introduce visible asterisks for each character typed at the sudo password prompt, ending a 46-year tradition of silent input. This change is enabled by default in the new Rust-based sudo implementation (sudo-rs) and aims to improve user experience despite some security concerns.
mainPoints: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS will show asterisks for each sudo password character typed, replacing the traditional silent input.
The original silent prompt was a security measure to prevent shoulder surfing by hiding password length.
The change is enabled by default in sudo-rs, a Rust rewrite of sudo, which Ubuntu adopted starting with version 25.10.
The security risk of showing password length is considered minimal by developers, who argue that shoulder surfing is unlikely to be mitigated by hiding asterisks.
Users can revert to the classic silent prompt by modifying the sudoers configuration.
This change is part of broader modernization efforts in Ubuntu 26.04, including GNOME 50 on Wayland, Linux kernel 7.0, and more Rust-based utilities.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are accurate and verifiable. The sudo utility was indeed created in 1980 with a silent password prompt, and Ubuntu has recently transitioned to sudo-rs, a Rust implementation. The decision to enable pwfeedback (showing asterisks) by default in sudo-rs is documented in upstream commits and bug reports. The security arguments presented reflect common discussions in the Linux community about usability versus security trade-offs. The ability to revert the behavior via sudoers configuration is standard practice. The timeline and technical details align with publicly available information about Ubuntu's development plans and sudo-rs changes.
considerations: The article does not mention potential impacts on accessibility, such as how the change affects users relying on screen readers or other assistive technologies.
It does not explore in detail the perspectives of security experts who might argue for maintaining silent input in high-security environments.
The discussion does not address whether similar changes are planned or implemented in other major Linux distributions beyond Linux Mint and Ubuntu.
The article does not provide direct quotes or perspectives from Canonical representatives or the sudo-rs development team beyond commit messages and bug report summaries.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Ubuntu 26.04's change to show password length feedback during sudo prompts, a shift from the traditional silent input. Opinions vary from seeing it as a positive UX improvement that modernizes long-standing quirks, to concerns about security risks such as revealing password length to observers or recordings. Some suggest alternative feedback methods that balance usability and security.

comments.author: JoshTriplett
comments.text:
I'm glad to see this change. This was already the case for GUI password prompts, and I'm happy to see terminals following suit.

This wasn't someone seeing Chesterton's fence and deciding to knock it down thoughtlessly. This is a change that someone can in fact think all the way through and say "yeah, this should be changed, it's an improvement and doesn't cause any meaningful reduction in security".
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and informed perspective, recognizing the change as a thoughtful improvement consistent with GUI behavior and not compromising security.

comments.author: dtech
comments.text:
This is such a good decision. It's one of those things that's incredibly confusing initially, but you get so used to it over the years, I even forgot it was a quirk.

In the modern world there is no plausible scenario where this would compromise a password that wouldn't otherwise also be compromised with equivalent effort.
comments.analysis: The comment acknowledges the initial confusion but correctly notes that the security impact is minimal given modern threat models.

comments.author: written-beyond
comments.text:
The number of times I've been stuck wondering if my keystrokes are registering properly for a sudo prompt over a high latency ssh connection.

These servers I had an account setup too were, from what I observed, partially linked with the authentication mechanism used by the VPN and IAM services. Like they'd have this mandatory password reset process and sometimes sudo was set to that new password, other times it was whatever was the old one. Couple that with the high latency connection and password authentication was horrible. You would never know if you mistyped something, or the password itself was incorrect or the password you pasted went through or got double pasted.

I think this is a great addition, but only if it leads to redhat adopting it which is what they were running on their VMs.
comments.analysis: Provides real-world context on usability issues with sudo password prompts over SSH, supporting the change as a practical improvement.

comments.author: pvillano
comments.text:
How much information is there in knowing the length of someone's password?

If we know the password's length, it saves us from guessing any shorter passwords. For example, for a numeric password, knowing the length is 4 saves us from having to guess [blank], 0-9, 00-99 and 000-999. This lowers the number of possibilities from 1111 to 1000. The password has 90% of it's original strength. A [0-9a-zA-Z] password retains 98% of it's original strength
comments.analysis: Accurately quantifies the security impact of revealing password length, showing it is a relatively minor reduction in entropy.

comments.author: andai
comments.text:
If the UX issue is "I don't know whether the keystroke registered", isn't there a way to fix it without revealing the length? e.g. I've seen some password inputs that display multiple dots per keystroke.

Though I guess the broader context is if the attacker has "shoulder-level access" you probably have bigger things to worry about ;)
comments.analysis: Offers a constructive suggestion to improve UX without compromising security by revealing password length.

comments.author: Tepix
comments.text:
Why not just display a single character out of a changing set of characters such as
    / - \ | 
(starting with a random one from the set) after every character entered?
That way you can be certain whether or not you entered a character but and observer can‘t tell how many characters your password has.
comments.analysis: Proposes a clever method to provide feedback while obscuring password length, balancing usability and security.

comments.author: dhsbdisnd
comments.text: Seems like a decision made by and for a generation that has no regard and no understanding for UNIX.
comments.analysis: An emotional and dismissive comment lacking constructive reasoning or evidence, reflecting generational bias rather than technical critique.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
They could have just made it an option to enable the new behavior. There was no need to change the default.

As for security: 'shoulder surfing' may not be as much of a concern, but watching a livestream or presentation of someone who uses sudo will now expose the password length over the internet (and it's recorded for posterity, so all the hackers can find it later!). They've just introduced a new vulnerability to the remote world.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid security concern about revealing password length during public presentations or recordings, though the practical risk may be limited.

comments.author: pessimizer
comments.text:
Silent sudo passwords are not a real problem. I wouldn't give up the slightest whiff of security over them. This is one of the things that I see that I have a minority position on, and it lowers my general opinion of humanity.

It's on brand for Ubuntu, though. They've been looking for an audience that is not me for a very long time. I sometimes worry about Debian's resistance to social pressure, though. It seems that Debian doesn't fall for marketing or corporate pressure, but they sometimes fall when they are surrounded by people who have fallen for marketing or corporate pressure.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong personal opinion prioritizing security over usability changes; while somewhat emotional, the security caution is reasonable.

comments.author: jiehong
comments.text: This fixes another issue with that if you make a typo in your password, you don't know how many characters you need to delete, but now you would.
comments.analysis: Highlights a practical usability improvement that helps users correct password input errors more easily.
